coredata.dc:title: Failure and constitutive behavior of a Li-ion pouch cell under mechanical loading
coredata.dc:creator: Trondl, Andreas
Schaufelberger, Benjamin
Kisters, Thomas
Fehrenbach, Clemens
Steiert, Anja
Sun, Dong-Zhi
coredata.dc:description: The constitutive mechanical behavior of the individual components in Lithium-ion cells has a fundamental influence on the development of internal electrical short-circuits in crash-relevant load scenarios. These short circuits can result in explosive, thermally unstable states (so called thermal runaways). The experimental characterization of mechanical properties of single components but also of entire cells is therefore a central aspect in the safety-related assessment of battery systems. This paper presents and compares experimental results of the mechanical characterization of individual cell components as well as whole pouch-cells under different loading patterns. Especially, the different mechanical behavior of the active materials NMC and graphite was investigated in dry and wet conditions. In compression tests, the presence of the electrolyte reduced the stress levels by about 100 % for the graphite layered anode (Cu) and by about 20 % for the NMC layered cathode (Al) compared to dry conditions. The separator displayed an anisotropy with tensile strengths differing by a factor of three between the longitudinal and transversal orientations. For investigating the failure of a whole pouch-cell, interrupted flat-punch and hemispherical-punch indentation tests were performed. Post-mortem CT analysis revealed that crack development is rather gradual than abrupt. The initiation and propagation of the failing cell structure were examined and related to the characteristics of the individual cell components. It could be concluded that for a physical based modeling of the deformation and fracture processes within the cell, understanding the mechanical behavior on component and on cell level is crucial.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery safety: failure sequence
Active materials
Electrolyte
Pouch cells
CT (computer tomography)
Fracture pattern
Fracture initiation
Inner structure of pouch cell
Indentation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigated cells, cell components and materials
Analysis of failure behavior and consequences for model development

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministerium für Wirtschaft, Arbeit und Tourismus Baden-Württemberg
State Ministry of Economic Affairs , Labor and Tourism Baden-Württemberg

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research received funding from the State Ministry of Economic Affairs , Labor and Tourism Baden-W\u00FCrttemberg (Ministerium f\u00FCr Wirtschaft, Arbeit und Tourismus Baden-W\u00FCrttemberg) through the project BATTmobil (a cooperative project between Fraunhofer EMI and Fraunhofer IWM) , grant number 3\u20134332.62-EMI/3 . Some parts of the work were financially supported by the Federal Ministry of Education and Research Germany (Bundesministerium f\u00FCr Bildung und Forschung, BMBF) in context of the associated follow-up project BATTmobil-2. The projects were part of the Tech Center i-protect.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tensile test of cell components: global stress-strain characteristics (left), experimental setup (upper mid), specimens (upper, lower mid) and supplied partially coated foils (lower right).
Compression test of electrodes: global stress-strain characteristics of active materials, experimental setup, cut foils for stacked specimens (upper row).
Punch test for individual foil components and stacked system: global force displacement characteristic (mid right), experimental setup (left), layered foils for stacked specimens (upper right).

Experimental results of interrupted penetration tests with flat punch. Top left: Force-intrusion curves. Top right: Selected planes for illustration of the failure behavior. Bottom: Illustration of CT-scans for different interrupted tests at selected planes.

Experimental results of interrupted penetration tests with hemispherical punch. Top left: Force-intrusion curves. Top right: Selected planes for illustration of the failure behavior. Bottom: Illustration of CT-scans for different interrupted tests at selected planes. Distance between y-plane and y∗-plane is roughly 4 mm.
Overview of performed tests, a-h are tests on component level, whereas i-j are tests on the full cell.
Layers and thicknesses of the investigated pouch cell, manufactured at Fraunhofer ISIT.

head.ce:title: Failure and constitutive behavior of a Li-ion pouch cell under mechanical loading

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Crash Safety and Damage Mechanics, Business Unit Component Safety and Lightweight Construction, Fraunhofer Institute for Mechanics of Materials IWM, Woehlerstraße 11, 79108, Freiburg, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Business Unit Component Safety and Lightweight Construction
Fraunhofer Institute for Mechanics of Materials IWM

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Crash Safety and Damage Mechanics, Business Unit Component Safety and Lightweight Construction, Fraunhofer Institute for Mechanics of Materials IWM, Woehlerstrasse 11, 79108 Freiburg, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The constitutive mechanical behavior of the individual components in Lithium-ion cells has a fundamental influence on the development of internal electrical short-circuits in crash-relevant load scenarios. These short circuits can result in explosive, thermally unstable states (so called thermal runaways). The experimental characterization of mechanical properties of single components but also of entire cells is therefore a central aspect in the safety-related assessment of battery systems. This paper presents and compares experimental results of the mechanical characterization of individual cell components as well as whole pouch-cells under different loading patterns. Especially, the different mechanical behavior of the active materials NMC and graphite was investigated in dry and wet conditions. In compression tests, the presence of the electrolyte reduced the stress levels by about 100 % for the graphite layered anode (Cu) and by about 20 % for the NMC layered cathode (Al) compared to dry conditions. The separator displayed an anisotropy with tensile strengths differing by a factor of three between the longitudinal and transversal orientations. For investigating the failure of a whole pouch-cell, interrupted flat-punch and hemispherical-punch indentation tests were performed. Post-mortem CT analysis revealed that crack development is rather gradual than abrupt. The initiation and propagation of the failing cell structure were examined and related to the characteristics of the individual cell components. It could be concluded that for a physical based modeling of the deformation and fracture processes within the cell, understanding the mechanical behavior on component and on cell level is crucial.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) are essential for reducing CO2 emissions in both private and public transportation. Policy decisions are driving the transition from internal combustion engines to BEVs over the next decades. As BEVs become more widespread, their safety becomes increasingly important. A critical safety issue is avoiding thermal runaway in crash situations. A thermal runaway, an exothermic reaction that releases heat, hot gases, and particles, can for example be caused by cell deformations [1–3].

Engineering design improvements for BEVs rely heavily on simulations of the entire vehicle structure, including Li-ion batteries. Due to computational constraints and the complex, layered nature of Li-ion battery cells, simplified and homogenized models are necessary [4–6]. These models, known as analogous models, are generally based on compressible plasticity models [7–9]. However, developing predictive analogous models requires a detailed understanding of deformation and failure behavior at meso- and micro-scale. Internal electrical short circuits, which can lead to thermal runaway, are often triggered by local mechanical failures of thin polymeric separator foils. The failure behaviors of Li-ion cells under various load cases, especially regarding local stress states and loading paths, are not fully understood [10,11].

Current analogous models often use oversimplified approaches based on relative nodal displacements [12] to predict short circuit, which inadequately approximate local mechanical conditions. A more refined approach could involve combining different local intrinsic mechanical quantities, such as volumetric strain and stress states, into a mechanical-based short circuit criterion [13]. Further inside into the deformation and failure behavior can also be given by detailed cell models [4,14–16]. However, for calibration and validation of these models’ experimental data at both the material and cell level is necessary.

The mechanical and electrical short-circuit behavior of pouch cells is determined by the material behavior of individual components and the structural layered cell setup. Compression-dominated loadings are primarily influenced by the granular active materials, which exhibit large volume changes due to their compressible nature. The stiffness of active materials is significantly lower than that of the metallic current collectors. Due to the thinness and relative independence of the metallic sheets, the bending stiffness [17,18] of the whole pouch cell is also quite low [19]. Tension-dominated loadings are mainly influenced by the stiffer current collectors [15]. Consequently, pure tension and compression experiments on individual materials characterize the deformation and failure behavior, impacting the properties of the entire pouch cell. Additional indentation tests with varying punch displacements were conducted to investigate the evolution of failure patterns in the layered cell structure using X-ray tomography.

Probably the most common way to characterize the deformation behavior of Li-Ion cells is measuring the force during punch intrusion (e.g. Refs. [11,20,21]). The recorded force-intrusion curve can be divided into three different phases, regardless of the geometry of the penetrator: The first stage ranges from the beginning to the maximum slope (inflection point), followed by the second stage to the maximum force, and the third stage is the force decay. Often, it is observed that the maximum force coincides with a short circuit (e.g. Ref. [22]) [22]. gives an attempted explanation for the three different stages based on hemispherical punch indentation in an NMC pouch cell. Stage one: compression of the stacked layers, neither cracking nor delamination. Stage two: adhesion between graphite coating and separator debonding of the interface between graphite coating and copper current collector, delamination of the separator however no failure. Stage three: short circuit along with a drop of force and failure of the layers from the top (impacted side) to the bottom.

[23] outline indentation tests with different punch geometries on an NMC pouch cell. Tests with flat, cylindrical (rod) and hemispherical (with different radii) punch geometries are shown. The tests were stopped immediately after reaching the force peak, afterwards mechanical cuts and cross section pictures were taken. Basically, all demonstrated cases show a distinct crack reaching from the top to the bottom (called as through-thickness failure) [21]. investigated the rate dependence of the failure behavior of a small scale NMC pouch cell under hemispherical and flat punch indentation loading, the investigated cells are from the same cell type as the ones investigated in the paper at hand. A comparison of post-mortem CT scans of tests stopped after reaching structural failure (i.e. after the force peak) illustrated the distinct rate dependence of the failure behavior. The rate dependence is explained by the squeezing of the electrolyte in the porous active materials. Dynamic failure of Li-Ion pouch cells is further investigated by Ref. [24] who explains the higher stiffness and lower peak force for higher loading rates with inertia effects of the layered structure [25]. states that the inflection point of the force-displacement curve goes along with the formation of a buckle around the indenter. It is also observed that failure mechanism is dependent on the loading velocity, with straight through-thickness failure for lower velocities and a damaged zone beneath the punch for higher velocities. Discussing the results from Refs. [23,25,26] supposes, that the failure mode is also dependent on the thickness of the cell or rather on the ratio between thickness and punch diameter. The presented results also complement several recent articles that have investigated the crashworthiness of lithium-ion cells by linking material behavior to system-level effects (see, e.g. Refs. [27–31]).

While many publications address specific aspects of Li-ion cells [11], this work aims to holistically investigate the macro and microscopic behavior of a pouch cell. The paper at hand focuses on the mechanical behavior of individual components (metallic current collectors, separator foils, and active materials) and highlights the different deformation and strain-rate properties of graphite and NMC active materials in dry and wet conditions. Another focus is the separation of the mechanical behavior of the coated anodic and cathodic layer system, which is not sufficiently represented experimentally in the literature. These findings are crucial for calibrating material models that better predict failure and short circuit. The experimental results on cell level provide a deeper understanding necessary to validate detailed Li-ion cell models especially with regard to the failure sequence of the components.

This experimental work is part of a cooperative project between Fraunhofer institutes IWM and EMI, targeting finite element model development for Li-ion battery cells (BATTmobil). The paper complements dynamic structural tests conducted on the same cells [21], providing a unique database for model development [8,9,15,19].

The experimental study includes unique tests for unveiling the influence of the liquid electrolyte, and a detailed analysis of quasi-static impact tests revealing the failure sequence of these cells, both is presented for the first time.

The entire test matrix includes test on component level (a-h) and tests on the full cell (i and j): Tensile tests on electrodes and separator (a-c), compression tests (d-g) and punch tests (fixed clamping in a ring-shaped matrix) on individual (a-c) and stacked electrodes (d-h) as well as standard indentation tests on pouch cells (i-j) with different indenters and intrusion depths. Compression tests were carried out for slow and fast load speeds. The separator film was tested in tension under longitudinal and transversal direction.

In addition, to clarify the material specifications: the copper foil used as the anode substrate is coated on both sides with graphite, the aluminum foil employed for the cathode is also coated on both sides with NMC. This precise distinction in material preparation is important for interpreting the mechanical behavior observed in the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigated cells, cell components and materials
ce:section.ce:para: From the demand to have a sound basis for model calibration and validation, the requirements were determined (i) sufficient number of tests at cell and material level, (ii) necessary knowledge about the cell design and manufacturing process, and (iii) posttest CT analysis. Therefore, specially manufactured pouch cells were used.

The pouch cells were designed and fabricated by Fraunhofer ISIT in a small series with standard NMC-graphite technology (NMC 622). Individual cell components were provided separately by the Fraunhofer ISIT (Fig. 1 lower right).

The interior of the cells contains of rectangular sheets of stapled anodes and cathodes electrically isolated by the (folded) separator. The electrode sheets have lateral dimensions of 100 mm × 65 mm, the separator layers being slightly larger to prevent contact between the electrodes at the electrode rims. An aluminum-based pouch cover shields the whole stack (see top right in Fig. 4).

The cells comprise overall 49 inner sheets, 12 cathodes, 13 anodes and 24 separator sheets. The anodes and cathodes are metal foils (current collectors) coated on both sides by active material. The separator is based on ceramic-coated PET material (Freudenberg FV1D). Including the cover foil on both sides of the cell, the whole setup consists of 101 material layers, listed in Table 1
together with their measured thicknesses. More details of the cells can be found in Ref. [21] (see Table 2).

Summing up the thicknesses of all layer's results in an overall thickness of the cell of 6.485 mm, slightly lower than the measured overall cell thickness of (7 ± 0.5) mm. This discrepancy is due to unavoidable small gaps between the layers inside the cell and typical for such cell setups. The cells weight was (112 ± 2) g, the length was ∼100 mm and the width ∼65 mm.

In preparation of the tests, at least three at most five, full charge/discharge cycles were run with the cells. Afterwards the cells were discharged to the nominal end-of-discharge-voltage, which represents a state of charge (SOC) of 0 % (no deep discharge). This was the SOC level applied in all tests. It is believed that an SOC of 0 % does not affect the validity of the tests while reducing the hazards of toxic leakage and thermal runaway (cp [32,33].), allowing further post-test analysis of the deformed cell. The mean capacity of the cells was 3.2 Ah, the measured maximum deviations from this value amounted to 0.3 Ah.

A servo-hydraulic testing machine was used for all tests. Depending on the load level, calibrated load cells for maximum forces of ±500 kN or ±100 kN were used. The measurement of displacements was carried out locally by the optical system ARAMIS from gom. The recorded signal variables (force, electrical cell voltage) were processed with an HBM QunatumMX840a measuring amplifier.

To investigate the deformation and failure behavior of the main components in the Li-ion-cells (metallic current collectors, separators, and active materials) three different characteristic loadings, namely uniaxial tension, uniaxial compression and biaxial tension were carried out. The very thin samples were cut out of the available film material using a water jet process. The thin and partially coated foils were placed between two stiff cover plates during the water jet cutting to avoid very small possible edge cracks on the finished samples, which eventually could influence the initial failure behavior during testing.

Since only the electrodes (with active material coated metallic current collectors) and the separator films can carry appreciable tensile loads, only these materials were tested under tension. The active materials can essentially not transmit any appreciable tensile stress, because they substantially are granular medias with small intrinsic cohesion. As shown in Fig. 1 (lower right) the (dry) electrodes were supplied. Therefore, the tensile tests were performed on coated specimens only. For this reason, to calculate the tensile stress within the metal foils, the metal foil thickness was used as a reference, thus assuming the active materials do not carry tensile forces.

The tension test setup and the sample geometry are shown in Fig. 1. Due to the low forces, a frictional clamping of the foils is sufficient. To increase the probability of failure initiation in the middle of the measurement range, a slight sample taper (with a radius of ∼500 mm) was provided (see Fig. 1). The experiments were carried out under quasi-static conditions, with the test speed of 1 mm/min under nearly uniaxial tension.

Due to the high proportion of the active materials in Li-ion cells, precise characterization is of considerable importance. In addition, only a few studies on the deformation behavior of the active materials (LiMOx, graphite) commonly used in Li-ion cells can be found in the literature (e.g. Ref. [7]). As mentioned, the active material has nearly no (or negligible) influence for tensile dominated loadings. Contrary to this, for pressure dominated loadings the active material mainly determines the deformation behavior, because the metallic current collectors and the separator polymer are nearly incompressible and the stress levels for moderate deformation is significantly higher. Accordingly, the active material determines the deformation behavior under uniaxial pressure loads of pouch battery cells.

To determine the compressive behavior of LiMOx and Graphite (as well as its electrolytical suspensions) separately, compression tests with stacks of anode and cathode respectively were performed. In addition, the electrodes active materials were impregnated with a surrogate electrolyte liquid (anhydrous organic solvent PC with low-viscosity DMC as an additive to increase Li-ion mobility; no Li conducting salt due to toxic-vapors during testing) for different exposure times to investigate the transient influence of electrolyte diffusion (transition from compacted granular medium to suspension leads to different material properties) on the compressive behavior (impregnated electrodes).

The experimental setup for the compression test is shown in Fig. 2 . The specimen for compression consists of ten identical electrodes with quadratic cut, to provide a sufficiently big portion of the investigated active material. The cross section of cylindrical punches is at least 50 % smaller in comparison to the quadratic cut of the stacked coated metallic foils. The deformation is measured locally by using a clip gauge. The investigations were carried out with dry and wet (impregnated and diffused electrolyte) active materials. The punch testing speeds are nearly quasistatic (1 mm/min) and moderate dynamic (100 mm/min).

To investigate the deformation and failure behavior for higher triaxialities (stress state close to tensile plane strain state), so-called punch tests were carried out. For this individual foils or foil stacks are clamped between a perforated die and a perforated hold-down device. The clamped test foil, in the case at hand a stacked foil system, was loaded by a spherical punch in the circular openings of the upper and lower dies. For a quantitative analysis force and displacement of the punch are recorded. The experimental setting is intended to mimic loading pattern from typical crash scenarios.

The basic test setup can be seen in Fig. 3
(based on DIN EN ISO 20482) with the servo-hydraulic testing machine (INSTRON 1275) and the used 3D camera system for local strain measuring.

Besides testing the single materials of the Li-Ion-pouch cell, indentation tests with different punch geometries were executed with active cells. These cell tests are intended to experimentally investigate the deformation and failure behavior as well as the associated electrical short-circuit behavior of the entire (intrinsic) cell structure, which is why the tests were terminated at different indentation depths (interrupted indentations represents different loading levels). The tested cells with different indentation levels were then additionally examined with CT scans for internal failure patterns and their load level-dependent development.

Indenters with flat and hemispherical punch heads, both with a diameter of 20 mm, were used. The quasi-static tests were conducted on a servo-hydraulic testing machine (of brand MTS) with loading capacity of 100 kN. The punch displacement was measured locally and compared to the integrated machine measurement, which shows negligible deviations related to the lower punch forces. An HBM Quantum MX840a measuring amplifier was used to determine the force, the punch displacement, and the cell voltage.

The intrusion tests were performed with a punch velocity of 0.01 mm/s. Due to this low velocity it was possible to stop the testing at a specific intrusion. To analyze the actual state of the inner deformations and possible damage a CT-scans of the damaged cells were performed.
All tests were done with a state of charge (SOC) of 0 % of the cells.

Subsequent CT-scans were performed to analyze the failure behavior of the punch loaded cells. For the scans which were performed at Fraunhofer EMI, a Waygate Phoenix v|tome|x M300 having a dual tube scanner with 300 kV micro and 180 kV macro focus was used. The tube was operated at 200 μA and 36 W for 500 ms exposure time with 3000 projections for each sample. A resolution of 27.64 μm could be achieved.

In the scans, due to the higher absorptivity, the anodes (copper and graphite) are the brightest layers. Cathodes and the pouch cover can also be identified, which, unfortunately, is not the case for the separator. It should be kept in mind that each test was performed on an individual cell. Therefore, by comparing one intrusion depth with another, not a subsequent stage of the former crack pattern is seen. Furthermore, the deformation states are shown after unloading.

In the following subsections the essential experimental results for the different loadings are presented and shortly discussed. Strains and stresses illustrated in the following diagrams correspond to the measured extension related to the initial gauge length and the measured force related to the primary most heavily loaded initial area respectively.

The single-layer results serve as mechanistic building blocks: they reveal intrinsic poro-mechanical responses and interfacial slip phenomena that also govern local stress distributions in the full cell. In 3.1.3/3.2 we further reconcile these findings with the CT-observed failure patterns in the true multi-layer architecture, which was also addressed to Ref. [34] from this publication.

The stress strain curves show large differences in the tensile strengths (maximal stresses) as well as in the failure strains (final fracture), which illustrates Fig. 1 (left). The scatter of the measured characteristics for each individual material (component) lies in an expected and acceptable range. The stiffness (slope of the force curves in Fig. 1 left) of the individual materials also vary over a wide range. The separator film shows a significant anisotropy, with the tensile strengths differing by a factor of three.

As expected, the measurements of the metallic current collectors show significantly higher stiffness and tensile strength compared to the separator and the pouch cover (bag). In addition, the separator and the pouch cover have significantly larger fracture strains than the electrodes made from copper and aluminum.

The low fracture strain of the aluminum cathode in comparison to the copper anode is remarkable. For aluminum alloys the fracture strains are normally at least above 2–4 % (0.02–0.04), which is significantly higher in comparison to the measured values (below 0.5 % or 0.005, shiny green curves). It is expected that the low fracture strain of the cathode is most probably related to the coating, consisting of possible harder particles which induces small initial defects on the metallic surface during the process of calendering (coating, consisting of LiMOx and additional unknown additives, is joined under high pressures to thin Al-foils). This circumstance is not observed for the Cu-anode due to the rather soft graphite-based active material. The explanation for the low fracture strains is supported by the fact that tensile specimens made from identical uncoated aluminum foils have been measured to have much higher elongations at fracture.

The observed separator foils show significantly different fracture behavior in axial and transversal direction. This separator foil is investigated in subsection 3.2 (Cell Tests) within the real layered cell structure during indentations. Since the separator is the central element for the intrinsic spatial separation between anode and cathode, a mechanical failure in most cases leads to a short circuit and with, dependent on the loading state, may result in a thermal runaway. A high failure strain of the separator is therefore generally advantageous to reduce the probability of electrical short circuits.

The compaction behaviors of the anodic and cathodic active materials are significantly different, which shows Fig. 2 upper row. For similar anodic and cathodic compaction strains between 0.4 and 0.5, the first stress maxima differ by 150 MPa which is around 50 %. Additionally, there is also a difference between dry active materials, and such being impregnated with electrolyte. The difference between dry and wet is significantly higher for the graphite based anodic material. Note, that due to the behavior in Fig. 2 for 1 mm/s shows no differences for both impregnation-times (1.7 h, 5 h; saturation of electrolyte), an analog comparison at 100 mm/s was omitted.

The loading speed shows an influence on the deformation and failure behavior of the impregnated stacked coated foil system. This strain rate effect is not really uniform for both investigated layered systems but shows in a simplified manner a decrease of the stress levels for higher deformation speeds (100 mm/min) in comparison to quasistatic conditions (1 mm/min). For non-impregnated stacks, the influence of the loading speed on the stiffness lies – for the investigated velocities – within the experimental scatter.

In general, the presence of the electrolyte in the active material reduces the stress level (see Fig. 2 upper row), whereby a similar behavior was observed in Ref. [35]. The liquid electrolyte essentially causes a reduction in friction when the particles slide in the active materials, making them easier to deform under external loads, even if the particles were pressed together under high pressure during calendaring (diffusion of electrolyte between particle boundaries). The effect of the electrolyte on the rate dependence of the mechanical behavior on material level, which was derived from the two groups shown (dry & wet 5 h at 1 mm/min vs. 100 mm/min), is consistent with the results on cell level shown in Ref. [34], where very similar pouch cells were dynamically tested.

The liquid filling of the pores constitutes an additional resistance to compression as the incompressible liquid is pushed out of the pores before a further compression of the porous material is possible, a process that is highly strain-rate dependent. It is interesting to see that for the anode stiffening and peak force reduction with increasing velocity (also observed in Refs. [21,34,36,37]) is much more pronounced then for the cathode side. On the cathode side, surprisingly, these mechanisms seem less significant. Here, only negligible stiffening and altogether no peak force reduction is observed at all. In fact, the cathode does endure even larger strains at higher peak forces with increasing velocity.
Interrupted compression test of electrodes: detection of initial fracturing of electrodes (lower row).
Observing that the poro-mechanical effect is more pronounced for the anode, one can conclude that the anode dominates the strain-rate effect on the mechanical behavior of the overall cell.

To further understand the observed stress-strain curves, the fracture behavior under compression of the stacked impregnated electrodes was further, see Fig. 2 lower row. For this purpose, the compression tests were interrupted at certain characteristic load levels and the deformed stacks were visually observed for initial crack formation with a simple light microscope.

With this method it could be shown for the anode that the flattening of the stress-strain curve (at strain of ∼0.36) is not related to an initial crack formation in the impregnated anode foils (see left in Fig. 2 lower row). This behavior is properly in relation to the increased sliding of particles in the graphite-based active-material. The significant drop in force (stress) correlates directly with the formation of a straight in-plane crack over all layers of the stack of anode foils.

For the cathodic aluminum foils coated with LiMOx, the initial cracking (axial symmetric cracks at the edge of the punch) shows no immediate effects in the global measured stiffness characteristic (derivative of stress-strain curve), which is shown on the right of Fig. 2 lower row.

For the aluminum-based cathode, the observed initial fracture formation under compression correlates to the stress level for tensile testing (see green curve in Fig. 1 left), although the global loading state (tension, compression) is rather different. The copper-based anode does not show this behavior since the breaking stress under tension is significantly higher here. However, in principle it can be assumed that the crack formations, observed in the compression test, take place under shear-dominant stress states (cutting edges of cylindrical punch).

The measured force-displacement curves of punch tests for the whole stack of foils (stack layup: separator at punch side, anode, separator, cathode at top) as well as for the individual (electrodes are shown in Fig. 3 right. For this deformation pattern a biaxial dominated stress state is induced, which should be similar during indentations occurring in representative crash loading scenarios.

The initial higher stiffness of the foil stack (up to 2 mm punch displacement) is primarily and obviously dominated by the behavior the metallic current collectors (Cu and Al). The scatter of the first force peak is relatively small, related to the punch displacement at around 2 mm. Due to the higher initial stiffness this scatter leads to lager differences in the force peak value between 55 N and 75 N. After the initial drop of force (see remark “1. Fracture of electrodes” in Fig. 3 right), the structure of foil stack is principal determined by the strength of the still undamaged separator films. Therefore, the stiffness of the foil stack has changed to significant lower values.

Additionally, the deformation characteristics of the different individual foils correspond to the significant changes (drops, stiffnesses resp.) in the force curve of the foil stack. Especially the fracturing of the metallic current collectors and the separator foil fits quite well in relation to the whole foil stack (comparison of failure sequence in Fig. 3 with Fig. 1).

In the following results of flat cylindrical and hemispherical punch indentation tests are shown for different indentation depths. Force-intrusion curves are discussed, based on the subdivision in three stages (e.g. see Ref. [22]). The behavior of the curve is correlated with the respective failure pattern observed in post-mortem CT scans.

Fig. 4 shows the experimental results of the test campaign with a flat punch. Test FP-1 was stopped in stage one, FP-2 in stage two and FP-3 at the beginning of stage three. The comparatively high intrusion for FP-4 was chosen to clarify causes of the force decline. In these experiments, voltage drop occur noticeably before the force peak, almost in accordance with the inflection point. It shall also be mentioned that the lower stiffness of FP-4 compared the three other tests presumably results from an accidental storing of the cell at SOC 100 % for more than a year.

An illustration of CT-scans of FP-1 in Fig. 4 is omitted, since no layer failure could be detected in the CT. So, by considering that at FP-2 the top layer has already failed around the edge of the punch, it can be concluded that cracking is initiated for an indentation between 1.6 mm and 2.7 mm. By noting the inflection point at 2.2 mm, which goes along with a voltage drop, it appears likely that at this penetration the upper most separator and presumably also upper anode and cathode foils fail. Besides the axial symmetric crack along the edge of punch, at FP-3 also almost vertical cracks (in the y-plane illustrated in Fig. 4 ranging from the top to the bottom side) occur. This failure of the inner structure over the full thickness is often denoted as through-thickness failure. So, the crack planes of these two cracks are orthogonal to the y-plane and have an angle of roughly ±70 to the x-axis. The crack, presumably originated from the crack along the edge of the punch, propagate further to the bottom side of the cell. For the illustrated case (see FP-3 y-plane) the crack on the left side already reaches the bottom side of the cell and goes backward (“V-shape”) to the top, whereas the crack on the right side is less pronounced. By comparing this crack pattern with the crack pattern at the x-plane it can clearly be seen that that the failure behavior is no longer symmetric. For FP-4 further vertical cracks occur, so that in width direction a “W-shape” failure pattern format. Also, the crack opening is considerably larger than in FP-3. For the largest indentation analyzed within the present study (FP-4), the portion of the cell below the punch seems to have no structural connection with the outer part of the cell anymore (except for the pouch bottom-side which only failed with two cracks in width direction), so the internal stack is basically blanked out from the surrounding.

The force-intrusion and voltage-intrusion curves of the interrupted tests are shown in Fig. 5 : HP-1 was interrupted in stage one, HP-2 quite precisely at the inflection point (thus at the transition between stage one and stage two), HP-3 in stage two, HP-4 at the force peak (transition between stage two and stage three) and HP-5 after considerable decline of the force (stage three). In contrast to the flat punch indentation, the voltage drop goes along with force peak.

In the CT-scans of HP-1 and HP-2 no failure could be detected, hence an illustration of these two tests is omitted. Scans of HP-3 demonstrate that failure is initiated out of center (illustrated as y∗ plane, which is roughly 4 mm away from the center plane). The crack pattern looks like an upside-down V. At the illustrated state, the crack in the anodes can be seen clearly (at least six anode foils fail). Whether cathode layers already failed is less clear. Cathode layers seem to be thinner in in the vicinity of the cracks of the anodes, that might be due to necking or recent failure (without sufficient crack opening). The cracks, initiated at the top side then propagate further in thickness and width direction. It can be assumed, that cracking (stable crack growth) is the reason for the lower stiffness in stage two compared to stage one. At force maximum (HP-4) the crack goes from top to bottom, all internal layers have failed (y-plane of HP-4), however the pouch still seems to be intact. The crack pattern in HP-4 looks like an upstanding V. It is worth mention, that (as for flat punch indentation) distinct cracks occur in width direction (thus crack plane orthogonal to y-plane). In HP-5 the crack pattern (y-plane) is an upside-down V, thus similar to HP-3 but different to HP-4. Thereby it has to be kept in mind that three different cells-tests are observed, by considering that especially crack initiation is highly nonlinear, it is reasonable to assume, that the different appearance of the crack pattern in the y-plane is due to experimental scatter. Furthermore, noticeable in HP-5 is the considerable wider crack opening and pronounced bulging (already indicated in HP-4) at the backside. Also, failure of the pouch at top- and backside can be observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of failure behavior and consequences for model development
ce:section.ce:para: The observed through-thickness failure at force peak (FP-3 and HP-4) is in line with other experiments (e.g. Refs. [22,23,25]) for lower velocities [26]. assumes “the fracture exhibited an abrupt behavior” for quasi static loading, CT scans of the tests prior to the force maximum (FP-2 and HP-3) exhibit that failure of anode and cathodes occurs clearly before the force peak is reached. A very similar behavior is also observed in simulations models by Ref. [15], where the stress-strain curves from Figs. 1 and 2 were already applied in axis-symmetric finite-elements models to simulate hemispherical and flat cylindrical punch indentation. These models, though not fully able to reproduce the experimental failure behavior, clearly indicate that aluminum and copper, but also the separator fail under tension. Due to the distinct lower failure strain of aluminum and copper, these layers fail first. Though the buckle, which [25] note that it goes along with the inflection point, could be the consequence of failure of the metal foils in the upper part of the cell, which does not necessarily mean, that through-thickness failure is already at hand. From our perspective, force drop is the consequence of through-thickness failure. The exact shape of the crack pattern (i.e. V or upside-down V) most probably depends on the local stress state prior the crack initiation. Simulation results of detailed models, considering the different stiffnesses of the single components, demonstrate the complex stress states of Li-Ion cells during mechanical deformation [15]. For indentation, vertical compression, especially in the zone directly beneath the punch, is at hand. Furthermore, copper, aluminum and separator foils are under in-plane tension. Thus, failure is expected to occur, where at first a critical combination of the respective stresses (presumably shear) occurs. The distinct mechanism leading to separator failure, and thus short circuit must remain open, from the present analysis it is not clear, if separator failure is the consequence of through-thickness failure, or through-thickness failure is the consequence of separator failure. The former could e.g. be caused by immediate stress redistribution after through-thickness failure, for the latter assumption, the separator would be essential for preserving the mechanical integrity of the cell. Following this, for future simulation models, having the requirement to predict occurring forces and the force-drop, correct modeling of the failure sequence is essential. This includes the modeling of deformation and failure of single layers (as experimentally outlined in 3.1) and the transfer from component to stack level. Thereby, simulation results from Ref. [15] indicate that 3D models are necessary and special care has to be taken on crack propagation and the treatment of already failed layers.

In general, the material behavior of the individual components (metallic current collectors, separator, and active materials) can be well characterized experimentally despite the very low layer thicknesses of the individual layers. The main findings of the material tests are:
•
Expected material behavior for copper in tension and compression. Aluminum foils show lower fracture strain, most likely caused by surface defects (indentation of hard particles in NMC during calendering).
•
Compressive behavior of active materials determined using uniform stacking systems of the respective electrodes. A significant influence of the electrolyte (more pronounced at the anode, i.e. graphite, side) is detected.
•
Separator foils (polymer films) show a considerable (6–8 times) higher elongation at break compared to the metal films. They exhibit pronounced anisotropy, both for the tensile strengths and with respect to the fracture strain.
•
Puncture tests of the equivalent foil stacks underline the load carrying behavior of anodes and cathodes for lower indentation, whereas final fracture is determined by the separator.

Recapitulating the results from the two test configurations (flat and hemispherical punch) on cell level, in the following, an attempt is made to deduce some key findings and explanations for the observed failure behavior. Though, similar failure phenomena were observed for other cells, their sequence and consequences, e.g. on the force-intrusion curves, are to a certain extent cell specific.

The performed experiments clearly show that cracking is initialized before the force peak is reached. It seems that the inflection-point is a good indication for the initialization of layer failure. For further penetration (between inflection point and force maximum), failure is an ongoing process. The continuous crack-growth is assumed to result in a lower stiffness. CT scans show that force-peak go along with through-thickness failure of the cell interior structure. Though, the micromechanical mechanism causing the force-peak, or rather the decline of the force, are not fully understood yet. At this point, detailed simulation models, including the mechanical behavior on component level could give further inside into the distinct mechanical processes during crack formation.

Andreas Trondl: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Benjamin Schaufelberger: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis. Thomas Kisters: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Clemens Fehrenbach: Validation, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Anja Steiert: Writing – review & editing. Dong-Zhi Sun: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research received funding from the State Ministry of Economic Affairs, Labor and Tourism Baden-Württemberg (Ministerium für Wirtschaft, Arbeit und Tourismus Baden-Württemberg) through the project BATTmobil (a cooperative project between Fraunhofer EMI and Fraunhofer IWM), grant number 3–4332.62-EMI/3. Some parts of the work were financially supported by the Federal Ministry of Education and Research Germany (Bundesministerium für Bildung und Forschung, BMBF) in context of the associated follow-up project BATTmobil-2. The projects were part of the Tech Center i-protect.
We are thankful to Ralph Langkemper for the CT-scans and to André Töpel and Jürgen Kuder from Fraunhofer EMI for performing experiments.
